symbols: joined hands — covenant bond of marriage ordained by God
temple columns — sacred setting indicating divine sanction of the union
high priest — God's mediating authority over the holy covenant
gathered witnesses — the community of Israel affirming the betrothal
architectural threshold — the liminal moment before the Incarnation narrative begins
description: A sepia-toned drawing depicts a ceremonial scene inside a columned temple or portico. At the center, a priest stands on a raised step joining the hands of a young woman and an older bearded man, surrounded by a crowd of witnesses in flowing robes. The architectural setting features tall classical columns and an arched doorway in the background. Figures are arranged in a processional semicircle, some kneeling, others standing in attitudes of solemn attention. The delicate linework and warm wash give the composition a sketch-like, preparatory quality.
theological_description: The Betrothal of Mary and Joseph, drawn from the Protoevangelium of James and medieval tradition, represents the divinely orchestrated preparation for the Incarnation. Though not narrated in canonical scripture, Matthew 1:18 presupposes the betrothal ('before they came together'), and the event holds deep theological significance: God arranged that His Son would be born within a lawful marriage to protect both Mary's honor and the child's legal Davidic lineage (Matt 1:1-16). Joseph's role as guardian and legal father fulfills the prophecy that the Messiah would come from David's house (2 Sam 7:12-13). The temple setting emphasizes that this union is not merely social but liturgical -- a covenant act under priestly authority. Patristic writers (Jerome, Ambrose) stressed that Mary's virginity was preserved within marriage, making the betrothal a mystery of grace: the highest consecration coexisting with the conjugal bond.

scripture_refs.reason: States that Mary was betrothed to Joseph before the conception by the Holy Spirit -- the canonical basis for this scene.
Identifies Mary as a virgin betrothed to a man named Joseph of the house of David.
The genealogy establishing Jesus's Davidic lineage through Joseph, which the betrothal legally secures.
God's promise to David of an eternal throne, fulfilled through Joseph's Davidic line.
The virgin shall conceive -- the betrothal sets the stage for this prophetic fulfillment.
